issues: palette_divergence: candidate uses vivid saturated green + near-white highlights; peers are muted gray/brown/gold with no vivid hues
highlight_intensity: candidate has near-white specular bloom on bow limbs; peers show subtle 1-step highlight at most
color_temperature: candidate leans strongly cool-saturated (neon green); peers are neutral-to-warm muted tones
rendering_style: candidate reads as high-contrast painterly with chromatic edge; peers are flat pixel-art with restrained shading
outline_color: candidate outline blends into green body in mid-sections, reducing legibility vs peers clear dark border
charm_level: sharp high-contrast bow lacks the rounded, cohesive 'cute' quality present in helmet and weapon peers
reasoning: Peer set (helmets, daggers, spear) shares a tight muted palette — desaturated grays, warm golds, dark near-blacks — rendered in classic flat pixel-art with one highlight step and a uniform dark outline. The longbow candidate breaks on two primary axes: (1) PALETTE — vivid lime-green body with near-white specular highlights is the most saturated sprite in the sheet by a large margin, zero overlap with peer browns/grays; (2) HIGHLIGHT INTENSITY — the white glow on the bow limb tips reads as a rendering-style mismatch (peers never go to pure white). Secondary issues: outline continuity weakens on the green-heavy sections, and the overall 'charm' register is off — the bow feels like a different art pass. Item-type difference (bow vs helmets) is not penalised, but the colour and rendering divergence are fundamental.
